cards.front: Who created the C programming language and when?
cards.back: Dennis Ritchie created C in 1972 at Bell Telephone Laboratories.

cards.text: C was designed to be a {{c1::efficient}}, {{c2::portable}} language for {{c3::systems programming}} (like operating systems and compilers).

cards.back: Bjarne Stroustrup created C++ in 1979 (originally called "C with Classes"). It was renamed to C++ in 1983.

cards.text: C++ was designed to add {{c1::object-oriented programming}} features to C while maintaining {{c2::backward compatibility}} with C code.

cards.front: What does the C++ design philosophy "trust the programmer" mean?
cards.back: C++ gives programmers freedom and control but doesn't prevent them from doing dangerous things. The language trusts programmers to know what they're doing.

cards.concept: Why C++ is harder to learn than some other languages
cards.explanation:
C++ prioritizes performance and flexibility over ease of learning. It has:
  • Low-level memory control (pointers, manual memory management)
  • Many features and ways to do things
  • Undefined behavior that can cause subtle bugs
  • Backward compatibility with C
The language trusts programmers but doesn't prevent errors.
examples: int* p; *p = 5; // Undefined behavior - uninitialized pointer
int arr[5]; arr[10] = 0; // No bounds checking
delete p; delete p; // Double delete - undefined behavior

cards.text: C++ is standardized by the {{c1::ISO}} committee. Major versions include C++98, C++11, C++14, C++17, {{c2::C++20}}, and {{c3::C++23}}.

cards.front: Why was C++11 considered a major update to C++?
cards.back: C++11 added many significant features (auto, lambdas, move semantics, smart pointers) that modernized the language. It's considered the start of "modern C++".

cards.text: The ISO C++ committee now releases new C++ standards every {{c1::3 years}}, with the most recent being {{c2::C++23}}.

cards.front: What is the "zero-overhead abstraction" principle in C++?
cards.back: You don't pay performance costs for features you don't use. High-level abstractions should have no more overhead than hand-written low-level code.

cards.front: What types of applications is C++ commonly used for?
cards.back:
C++ is used for performance-critical applications:
  • Operating systems and drivers
  • Game engines and AAA games
  • High-performance servers
  • Embedded systems
  • Scientific computing and simulations
